title: Sumatra Coffee: Why It Tastes Like Cigars ☕📈
description:
Sumatra's unique wet-hulled process, called giling basah, creates the famous earthy, tobacco, and forest floor cup. The science, the small farms, and the green bean color that breaks every rule.

Sources: SCA Indonesia origin guide, James Hoffmann, Sumatran coffee research, Sprudge.

segments.voice: Sumatran coffee looks broken before you even brew it. The green beans are dark olive, almost blue, instead of pale jade. They are misshapen. Some look chipped. Specialty buyers worldwide would reject these beans as defective from any other origin. From Sumatra, they are exactly what people pay extra for.

The reason for this strange appearance is a processing method unique to Indonesia, called giling basah. It translates to wet hulling. The method evolved in Sumatra because farmers needed to sell coffee quickly during humid weather. The shortcut became a flavor signature.

Traditional washed processing takes ten to fourteen days. Cherries get depulped, fermented, washed, dried slowly. The beans reach eleven percent moisture content with the parchment layer intact. Then the parchment gets hulled off mechanically. This is the standard worldwide.

Giling basah inverts the timing. Sumatran farmers depulp the cherry, ferment briefly, then dry the parchment-covered bean to only thirty to forty percent moisture. While still wet, they hull off the parchment. The bare bean then dries the rest of the way exposed.

The result is visually shocking. The bean swells with absorbed moisture during hulling. It takes on a blue-green color from extended contact with humid air. The shape distorts. Defects look more pronounced. The bean looks bad compared to any washed coffee.

The cup, however, tastes unlike anything else. Sumatran coffee shows heavy body, low acidity, earthy notes, tobacco, cedar, dark chocolate, forest floor, sometimes mushroom. The flavor profile is divisive. People who love it become obsessed. People who hate it find it muddy and weird.

Why does giling basah create this flavor? The extended exposure to humid air during hulling and drying triggers microbial activity on the bean surface. Yeasts and bacteria not present in standard washed processing develop. The microbes produce compounds that contribute to the earthy and savory flavors.

Sumatran coffee grows mainly in two regions. Aceh province in the north produces Gayo coffee at altitudes from twelve hundred to seventeen hundred meters. North Sumatra province produces Mandheling coffee from the Lake Toba region at similar altitudes. Both regions use giling basah almost universally.

Gayo coffee tends slightly cleaner than Mandheling. The Aceh region has stricter cooperative quality control. Mandheling coffee tends fuller and earthier. Roasters often specify which region the lot comes from because the cups differ noticeably. Both can be excellent.

The varietals in Sumatra are mostly Typica descendants, modified by centuries of selection. Tim Tim and Bor Bor are local cultivars derived from a Timor hybrid. Ateng is another local selection. Most Sumatran farms grow a mixed planting, like Ethiopian farms.

Farm sizes in Sumatra are tiny. The average smallholder owns one to two hectares. Family farms pass down across generations. Cooperatives consolidate the harvest. Permata Gayo Cooperative in Aceh represents over twenty-five hundred farmers and exports specialty grade Gayo lots worldwide.

Drying time in Sumatra runs short by global standards. Combined parchment and bean drying takes four to seven days. Compare that to fourteen to twenty days for natural processed coffees. The compressed timeline is climate-driven: Sumatran humidity makes long drying impractical.

The shorter drying time means Sumatran coffees enter export warehouses at slightly higher moisture levels than the SCA recommended ten to twelve percent. Some lots arrive at thirteen percent. This affects storage stability. Properly handled, the coffee ages well. Mishandled, it goes musty quickly.

Defect rates in Sumatran coffee run higher than Latin American averages. Quaker beans, broken beans, and insect damage appear more frequently. The defect tolerance is built into the price. Hand sorting after import removes the worst defects. Quality control adds cost.

Roasting Sumatran coffee challenges roasters. The lower acidity and uneven moisture make development tricky. Most specialty roasters apply medium-dark roasts to Sumatran lots, pushing through first crack toward second. The longer roast brings out the spice and chocolate notes.

Brewing Sumatran coffee at home, French press works exceptionally well. The full immersion captures the heavy body. James Hoffmann recommends ratio one to fifteen, coarse grind, water at ninety-three Celsius, four minute steep. The cup ends up syrupy with strong tobacco notes.

Espresso from Sumatran beans produces intense, syrupy shots with low acidity. Sumatran coffee is the foundation of many traditional espresso blends, especially Italian-style dark roasts. Lavazza Crema e Gusto and Illy Dark Roast both contain significant Sumatran content.

Pour-over methods can work with Sumatran lots but require lighter roast and careful technique. Most consumers find the cup lacks the brightness that makes pour-over interesting. Sumatran shines in immersion and pressure brewing more than percolation.

Indonesia produces other notable coffees beyond Sumatra. Java Island produces Java Estate coffee with washed processing, completely different from Sumatran character. Bali Kintamani produces high-altitude lots with bright cups. Sulawesi Toraja uses giling basah and tastes similar to Sumatran.

Civet cat coffee, called kopi luwak, originated in Sumatra and Java. Civets eat coffee cherries and excrete the beans. Farmers traditionally collected the wild-civet droppings, processed the beans, and produced a unique coffee. Modern kopi luwak comes mostly from caged civets in poor conditions. Specialty buyers avoid it for ethical reasons.

Climate change pressures Sumatran coffee badly. The optimum altitude band is moving upward as temperatures rise. Many smallholder farms occupy land at risk of becoming too warm for arabica within twenty years. Indonesia might lose half its arabica production capacity by 2050.

Robusta dominates Indonesian coffee statistically. Indonesia is the third-largest coffee producer globally, but most of that volume is robusta from Lampung and Bengkulu. Arabica from Sumatra is the high-quality minority. The two coffees taste nothing alike but share the country of origin.

Roasters specializing in Sumatran coffee include Stumptown Coffee in Portland, Counter Culture in Durham, and Onyx Coffee Lab in Arkansas. They source through direct relationships with cooperatives. Expect to pay between eighteen and thirty dollars per twelve ounce bag for quality Sumatran lots.

The aging potential of Sumatran coffee is unique. Some Indonesian coffees age intentionally for years. Aged Mandheling, called Old Brown Java, sits in warehouses for two to three years. The aging mellows acidity further and develops molasses and dried fruit notes. The technique is centuries old.

But there is something specialty coffee buyers rarely emphasize. The flavor profile loved by Sumatran coffee enthusiasts is partly the result of poor processing by global specialty standards. Cleaner Sumatran coffee processed by washed methods tastes more like washed coffees from everywhere else. The earthy character is, in some sense, a beautiful accident.

If you want to try Sumatran coffee, start with a Gayo lot from Aceh, roasted medium. Brew with a French press, drink with a splash of cream if desired. The cup will smell like a wood fire and taste like dark chocolate with cigar tobacco. Next video, Brazilian natural pulp coffee and why it dominates the global commodity market. Subscribe.
